posts.title: Welcome to RenueWell
posts.excerpt: We're so glad you're here. RenueWell is built for you — a gentle companion for life after cancer treatment.
posts.content:
We built RenueWell because recovery deserves more than a pamphlet.

When treatment ends, the structured support of the cancer care system often steps back — but the symptoms, the emotions, and the uncertainty don't. We wanted to build something that could walk alongside people through that transition.

RenueWell is a daily health companion for people in remission. You can log how you feel, track your habits, and generate a clear report for your next appointment — all privately, on your device.

We're just getting started. Thank you for being here.

posts.title: Seeing Cancer to Strike It: Theranostics Arrives in Summit
posts.excerpt: For decades, oncologists have aimed their most powerful weapons at  cancer using maps that were, at best, approximate. A newer approach called theranostics, a fusion of 'therapeutics' and 'diagnostics,' tries to fix that by giving doctors a precise address before they target it with treatment.
posts.content:
For decades, oncologists have aimed their most powerful weapons—chemotherapy, radiation, surgery—at  cancer using maps that were, at best, approximate. A newer approach called theranostics, a tidy fusion of 'therapeutics' and 'diagnostics,' tries to fix that by giving doctors a precise address before they target it with treatment. At Overlook Medical Center in Summit, New Jersey, this approach now anchors one of the more sophisticated cancer programs in the region, treating prostate cancer, neuroendocrine tumors, and certain thyroid cancers with a degree of precision that older systemic therapies weren’t built for.




The logic behind it, as Overlook's director of nuclear medicine, James Cassuto, MD, PhD, has described it, comes down to a single idea: if we can see it, we can treat it. The 'seeing' half of that sentence happens first. A patient receives an injection of a small radioactive tracer engineered to latch onto a protein found almost exclusively on the surface of their specific cancer cells, a molecular fingerprint of sorts that is unique to that tumor type. A PET or SPECT scan then reveals exactly where that tracer has settled, confirming both that the cancer carries the right target molecule and precisely where in the body it’s hiding.



Only once that target is confirmed does the 'treating' half begin. A nearly identical molecule is infused into the bloodstream. Instead of a diagnostic tracer, it now carries a therapeutic radioactive isotope, almost always lutetium-177, chosen because of its balance of precision. The particles it emits travel, on average, only about half a millimeter through human tissue: far enough to destroy a tumor cell and its closest neighbors, nowhere near far enough to meaningfully damage organs or tissue further away. Lutetium-177 also gives off a faint trace of gamma radiation, which means the very same dose that's treating the patient can also be imaged, letting physicians confirm exactly where it ended up. Overlook recently became the first hospital in New Jersey to install equipment purpose-built for this kind of imaging, GE HealthCare's StarGuide SPECT/CT scanner, giving its clinicians a sharper, more detailed view of the drug's journey through the body than older scanners typically allow.



For prostate cancer, the targeting molecule is called PSMA-617, named for the protein it seeks out: prostate, specific membrane antigen, a marker found in striking abundance on most prostate cancer cells. Combined with lutetium-177, the resulting drug is sold as Pluvicto, which has shown positive results in trial data. As shown in the pivotal VISION trial, published in the New England Journal of Medicine in 2021, researchers tracked 831 men with advanced, treatment-resistant prostate cancer and found that adding the drug to standard care pushed median survival from 11.3 months to 15.3 months, while sharply lowering patients' overall risk of death during the study period. A few years later, the PSMAfore trial, reported in The Lancet in 2024, tested the same drug earlier in the disease course—before chemotherapy rather than after—and found it more than doubled the time before tumors progressed, from 5.6 months to 11.6 months. Together, these two studies have moved Pluvicto from a treatment of last resort towards more of a frontline option.



A related drug, Lutathera, runs similarly against neuroendocrine tumors, a family of cancers known for growing slowly but resisting most conventional treatment. Here the targeting molecule is DOTATATE, which binds to a different surface protein, the somatostatin receptor, commonly found on neuroendocrine tumor cells. According to research from the NETTER-1 trial, published in NEJM in 2017, patients who received the drug fared dramatically better than those on high-dose hormone therapy alone: twenty months in, roughly two out of three treated patients had not progressed, compared with barely one in ten in the comparison group.



None of this should be mistaken for a cure. The survival gains documented in these trials are measured in months, not years, and theranostics only works for tumors that actually express the right molecular target in the first place. Still, for patients who have already exhausted chemotherapy, hormone therapy, and surgery, with little left to try, an extra year of meaningfully better odds is no small thing. And it's worth pointing out that this isn't an experimental therapy tucked away at some distant academic medical center, accessible only through a clinical trial waitlist. It's running now, on a working scanner, at our local Overlook hospital in Summit.




From Welsh, J. (2025, April 29). How is Theranostics Impacting Cancer Care?. InventUM. https://news.med.miami.edu/how-is-theranostics-impacting-cancer-care/ and What is PLUVICTO® (lutetium lu 177 vipivotide Tetraxetan)?. What Is PLUVICTO® (lutetium Lu 177 vipivotide tetraxetan)? (n.d.). https://us.pluvicto.com/about-pluvicto/how-pluvicto-works
